fields.name: 1. Civic education in schools should be a core subject
fields.content-01:
1. Introduction & warm-up (15 minutes)

‍

  • Icebreaker activity (5 minutes):
    • Ask students to define civic education in one sentence and share their answers with the class.
    • Discuss common themes that emerge from their definitions.
  • Group discussion (5 minutes):
    • Pose the question: "Why is civic education important in society?"
    • Encourage students to share personal experiences or examples where knowledge of civic education was beneficial.
  • Quick poll & Team formation (5 minutes):
    • Conduct a digital class poll: "Civic education should be a mandatory subject in schools"
    • Display poll results and briefly discuss initial reactions.
    • Divide students into two teams: Affirmative (For) and Opposition (Against).

‍‍

2. Research and argument development(20 minutes)

‍

  • Online research (10 minutes):
    • Students use their devices to research civic education, looking for key facts, case studies, and expert opinions.
    • Encourage students to gather statistics, examples, and arguments that support their assigned stance.
  • Structuring arguments (5 minutes):
    • Each team organizes their research into 3-5 strong arguments supporting their position.
    • Encourage logical structuring: introduction, main points, supporting evidence, counterarguments, and conclusion.
  • Role assignment & strategy planning (5 minutes):
    • Each team assigns roles: Lead Speaker, Supporting Debaters, Rebuttal Expert, and Closing Speaker.
    • Teams discuss strategy and anticipate potential counterarguments from the opposing side.

‍‍

3. Debate structure & guidelines (15 minutes)

‍

  • Explaining the debate format (5 minutes):
    • Opening statements (2 minutes per team).
    • Main arguments (3 minutes per speaker).
    • Rebuttals (2 minutes per team).
    • Closing statements (2 minutes per team).
  • Debate etiquette & expectations (5 minutes):
    • Emphasize respect for differing opinions and active listening.
    • Explain the importance of clear communication and well-supported arguments.
    • Remind students about time limits and the importance of staying on topic.
  • Assigning debate officials (5 minutes):
    • Designate a timekeeper and judges.
    • Review the debate rubric and scoring criteria.

‍

4. Debate session(30 minutes)

‍

  • Opening statements (4 minutes total):
    • Each team presents their stance and briefly outlines their key arguments.
  • Main argument presentations (12 minutes total):
    • Each team takes turns presenting structured arguments with supporting evidence.
    • Encourage speakers to be clear, persuasive, and engaging.
  • Rebuttal round (8 minutes total):
    • Each team counters the opposition’s points with logical rebuttals.
    • Students should highlight weaknesses in the opposing arguments while reinforcing their own stance.
  • Closing statements (4 minutes total):
    • Each team summarizes their position and makes a final persuasive appeal.
  • Judges deliberation & audience reflection (2 minutes):
    • Judges take a moment to assess team performance based on the debate rubric.
    • Audience members (non-debaters) reflect on which arguments they found most convincing.

‍

5. Debrief & reflection (15 minutes)

‍

  • Class discussion (5 minutes):
    • Ask: "Which arguments were the most convincing? Why?"
    • Encourage students to share their takeaways from the debate experience.
  • Peer feedback (5 minutes):
    • Students provide constructive feedback on their teammates’ and opponents’ performances.
    • Focus on clarity, delivery, and use of evidence.
  • Written reflection (5 minutes):
    • Students write a short paragraph reflecting on what they learned about debate, public speaking, and civic education.
    • Possible prompts: "What was the most challenging part of the debate?" "How can I improve my argumentation skills?"

‍

Tips and tricks

‍

  • Encourage students to use real-life examples, statistics, and historical events.
  • Set clear rules for respectful debate (no interruptions, personal attacks, or off-topic points).
  • Provide sentence starters for students who struggle with forming arguments.
  • Offer small incentives for the best speaker or most persuasive argument.
  • Incorporate multimedia elements (videos, news articles) to enhance engagement.
  • Encourage students to anticipate the opposition’s arguments and prepare counterpoints.
  • Have a practice round before the main debate to build confidence.
  • Allow students to switch sides for a second debate to understand multiple perspectives.
  • Provide constructive feedback and celebrate strong performances to boost confidence
fields.content-02:
Objectives

‍

Students will be able to develop strong argumentation and public speaking skills through structured debate on civic education in schools.
‍

Timeline

‍

  1. Introduction & warm-up (15 minutes)
  2. Research & argument development (20 minutes)
  3. Debate structure & guidelines (10 minutes)
  4. Debate session (30 minutes)
  5. Debrief & reflection (15 minutes)
fields.content-03:
Materials and tools

‍

  • Smartboard or projector for digital presentations.
  • Access to online research materials, including articles and case studies.
  • Digital debate structure and rubric handouts (shared electronically).
  • Mobile timer or countdown app for structured debate rounds.
  • Notebooks for brainstorming and note-taking.
fields.content-04:
Learning outcomes

‍

  • Clearly define civic education and its impact on society.
  • Present well-structured arguments for and against civic education as a core subject.
  • Utilize critical thinking skills to evaluate different perspectives.
  • Demonstrate active listening, collaboration, and rebuttal techniques.
  • Reflect on how debate skills contribute to professional and personal growth.
fields.content-05:
Assessment

‍

Students will be assessed based on the following criteria:

  1. Clarity & Organization (20%) - Arguments are logically structured and easy to follow.
  2. Evidence & Support (20%) - Use of facts, examples, and credible sources.
  3. Rebuttal & Counterarguments (20%) - Ability to respond effectively to opposition points.
  4. Public Speaking Skills (20%) - Confidence, articulation, eye contact, and voice projection.
  5. Engagement & Teamwork (20%) - Collaboration within the team and active participation.

‍

‍

Total Score 

  • 13-15 points: Outstanding performance – excellent execution across all criteria.
  • 9-12 points: Solid performance – some areas need improvement, but overall good work.
  • 5-8 points: Satisfactory – significant improvements needed in several areas.
  • Below 5 points: Needs substantial improvement – major issues across most areas.
